metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] AC4: Replace 'verified once manually' with an automated, re-runnable proving command (e.g., a tempdir test that makes compose-up.sh non-executable and asserts check 5 exits non-zero with 'could not enumerate provisioned volumes') and integrate it into `make config-check` or a named test target so the result is a durable CI artifact.
findings.finding: AC4's proving command is 'verified once manually during implementation and noted in the change message' — this is a one-time human attestation with no automated or repeatable artifact. The enumeration-failure path (compose-up.sh non-executable → loud failure) cannot be re-verified after landing, leaving the critical safety net unproven durably.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria / AC4 (enumeration failure is loud)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sub-test within check 5's self-test block: copy compose-up.sh to a tempdir, chmod a-x it, point the check at the copy, run check 5, assert exit non-zero and output contains 'could not enumerate provisioned volumes'. This runs on every `make config-check` alongside AC1's self-test and produces a durable, repeatable artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `--print-volumes` argument check must use a `set -u`-safe dereference (e.g., `[[ ${1:-} == --print-volumes ]]`) and add an AC or note confirming that `bash compose-up.sh` with no arguments (normal production invocation) still executes the full provisioning path without error after the new branch is inserted.
findings.finding: The plan adds a `--print-volumes` branch to compose-up.sh which already has `set -euo pipefail` (line 13). Under `-u`, a bare `$1` reference when the script is called with no arguments (the normal production invocation) triggers 'unbound variable' and aborts the script before any provisioning runs. The plan asserts the branch is placed 'immediately after variable/helper definitions' but does not specify that `$1` must be dereferenced safely (e.g., `${1:-}` or `[[ ${1:-} == --print-volumes ]]`), leaving a capability gap: a naive implementation breaks the no-arg production path.
findings.location: What (mechanism) / --print-volumes mode section; compose-up.sh line 13 (`set -euo pipefail`)
findings.suggested_fix: In the plan's --print-volumes mode section, replace 'when $1 == --print-volumes' with 'when ${1:-} == --print-volumes' and add to AC3 (or a new AC5): 'bash infra/scripts/compose-up.sh (no args) with stub executables completes the provisioning loop without error — confirming the new $1 branch does not abort under set -u.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `--print-volumes` derivation helper must be written to guarantee exit 0 for all valid `SITE_SUBDIRS` values (e.g., use `echo`, `printf`, or `tr` without grep-style commands that return 1 on no-match), so that a non-zero exit from `--print-volumes` always indicates a real invocation failure, not a pipefail triggered by the helper's own logic.
findings.finding: The plan requires that a non-zero exit from `compose-up.sh --print-volumes` is treated as a hard FAIL ('could not enumerate provisioned volumes'), but does not require the derivation helper to be pipefail-safe. Under `set -euo pipefail` (already active in compose-up.sh), any sub-command in the helper that returns non-zero — e.g., a `grep` that finds no match, a `sed`/`tr` on unexpected input, or a loop body command that fails — will cause `--print-volumes` to exit non-zero. This triggers 'could not enumerate' rather than 'volume X is missing', making a pipefail-triggered exit indistinguishable from a genuine invocation failure and causing permanent gate breakage instead of a useful drift report.
findings.location: What (mechanism) — `--print-volumes` mode and helper function specification
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'What (mechanism)' section: 'The helper function must be pipefail-safe: all commands within it must return 0 for any valid subdir name (use `printf` or `echo` for output, `tr` for character substitution — never `grep` or `sed` patterns that return 1 on no-match). Verified by AC3's stub-PATH test, which must not rely on side-effecting binaries that can mask a pipefail.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly enumerate the final SITE_SUBDIRS contents in the plan (e.g. `git index cache db etc logs plugins reviewbot reviewbot-tickets`), and clarify whether this plan or e991 is responsible for adding `reviewbot-tickets` to the list.
findings.finding: The plan does not enumerate the final contents of SITE_SUBDIRS anywhere. The existing loops contain `git index cache db etc logs plugins reviewbot` (8 entries), but docker-compose.yml declares 9 external volumes including `gerrit_reviewbot_tickets` (subdir `reviewbot-tickets`). The plan only mentions `gerrit_reviewbot_tickets` in AC3 as an example output, never specifying that both `reviewbot` and `reviewbot-tickets` must appear in SITE_SUBDIRS after the refactor.
findings.location: What (mechanism) section — 'hoist the currently-duplicated hardcoded subdir list into ONE top-level variable (SITE_SUBDIRS)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC3) that verifies both script headers contain the documented `--print-volumes` consumer/producer annotation — e.g. `grep -q 'consumed by config-check.sh check 5' infra/scripts/compose-up.sh` and the symmetric line in config-check.sh — so the interface contract cannot be silently dropped.
findings.finding: The plan describes a concrete deliverable — documenting the `--print-volumes` contract in both script headers (compose-up.sh: 'consumed by config-check.sh check 5'; config-check.sh: 'requires compose-up.sh --print-volumes') — but no acceptance criterion verifies that this documentation is present. An implementer could omit the headers and all four ACs would still pass.
findings.location: What section ('The --print-volumes contract is documented in both script headers'); AC1–AC4
findings.suggested_fix: AC5 (contract documented): `grep -q 'consumed by config-check.sh' infra/scripts/compose-up.sh && grep -q 'requires compose-up.sh --print-volumes' infra/scripts/config-check.sh` exits 0 — run as part of `make config-check` or as a standalone lint step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Capture the `--print-volumes` inter-script contract (rationale, side-effect-free guarantee, and consuming caller) in a discoverable location such as `CLAUDE.md`, a `docs/adr/` entry, or an `infra/scripts/README.md` — not only in per-script headers.
findings.finding: The --print-volumes inter-script contract is a novel architectural pattern for this repo (a machine-readable mode added to a side-effecting provisioning script, consumed by a separate check script), but the plan only documents it in per-script headers — no ADR, CLAUDE.md entry, or design-doc update is planned. Future maintainers adding new external volumes or new check scripts may not discover this contract exists or understand why the mode was designed to be side-effect-free.
findings.location: What (mechanism) section — '--print-volumes contract is documented in both script headers'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief entry to `infra/scripts/README.md` (or CLAUDE.md if that is the repo's convention) explaining: (1) compose-up.sh supports `--print-volumes` as a side-effect-free introspection mode, (2) config-check.sh check 5 depends on this mode, (3) any future provisioning script that adds external volumes must also implement or extend this mode. This makes the contract discoverable without requiring engineers to read both script headers simultaneously.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconsider whether `--print-volumes` needs to live in compose-up.sh at all: since config-check.sh already parses docker-compose.yml with python3+yaml (check 3), evaluate inlining the provisioned-volume derivation directly in config-check.sh (or a tiny shared data file) to eliminate the single-consumer inter-script subprocess contract.
findings.finding: The `--print-volumes` mode added to compose-up.sh has exactly one planned call-site (config-check.sh check 5), making it a single-consumer inter-script API baked into a boot orchestrator. The plan's own 'single source of truth' goal could be achieved without this cross-script contract: config-check.sh already opens docker-compose.yml with python3+yaml (check 3) and could derive the provisioned set by simply calling compose-up.sh with a side-effect-free flag — OR, more directly, config-check.sh could own a small `derive_volume_name(subdir)` helper and a shared subdirectory list (or read it from a tiny data file), keeping both the extraction logic and the comparison entirely within the script that already reads the compose file. Routing through compose-up.sh's $1-dispatch adds a versioned inter-script contract that must be maintained whenever compose-up.sh is refactored, with no second consumer in scope.
findings.location: What (mechanism) section — `--print-volumes` mode; compose-up.sh $1 dispatch; config-check.sh check 5
findings.suggested_fix: In config-check.sh check 5, derive the provisioned volume set directly from the same python3+yaml parse already done in check 3 (extract `volumes[name]['external'] == True` keys), and compare against a `SITE_SUBDIRS` array defined once at the top of config-check.sh (or sourced from a one-line data file). This eliminates the $1-dispatch mode from compose-up.sh entirely while still making SITE_SUBDIRS the single source of truth — with zero cross-script subprocess contract.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why the self-contained approach (deriving both the declared-external set AND the provisioned set in config-check.sh via a single python3+yaml pass, without invoking compose-up.sh --print-volumes) was considered and rejected in favor of the cross-script IPC contract — or adopt the simpler approach and remove the --print-volumes mode.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a bespoke '--print-volumes' inter-script IPC contract (compose-up.sh CLI → config-check.sh) without considering or rejecting the established simpler alternative: parsing docker-compose.yml on BOTH sides entirely within config-check.sh. Since config-check.sh already uses python3+yaml.safe_load to read the compose file (check 3), it could also reconstruct the volume-name derivation (gerrit_ + subdir, hyphen→underscore) in the same script, comparing declared-external vs. derivable-from-SITE_SUBDIRS with no cross-script CLI contract, no --print-volumes mode, and no coupling between the check and the provisioning script's argument interface.
findings.location: ## What (mechanism) section — '--print-volumes mode' description and 'single source of truth' claim
findings.suggested_fix: In config-check.sh check 5, parse docker-compose.yml for external volumes (already possible with yaml.safe_load — check 3's pattern) AND reconstruct the provisioned-volume names from an inline SITE_SUBDIRS list (mirrored from compose-up.sh's top-level variable), doing the full comparison in pure Python within config-check.sh. This eliminates the --print-volumes contract, the cross-script coupling, and the failure mode where a non-executable compose-up.sh silently affects the gate.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh` (and any other callers of `compose-up.sh`) as an unenumerated consumer in the plan, and confirm the no-argument invocation path is unaffected by the `--print-volumes` dispatch addition.
findings.finding: infra/scripts/autodeploy.sh is an unenumerated caller of compose-up.sh: it invokes compose-up.sh directly in the production deploy path, yet the plan never mentions it when describing the --print-volumes argument-dispatch change. If a future refactor of the $1 handling in compose-up.sh regresses the no-argument code path, autodeploy silently breaks.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence in the What section: 'autodeploy.sh calls compose-up.sh without arguments — the $1 dispatch guard must default-safe (e.g. `${1:-}`) so the no-arg path is unchanged.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
